decision.label: BOTTLENECK_NOT_EQUITY
decision.one_sentence: Transformer scarcity was real, but the cutoff evidence did not establish a material GEV transformer-to-fully-diluted-per-share free-cash-flow bridge.

funded_demand.qualification: Funded downstream infrastructure was verified, but no transformer supplier allocation was inferred from total data-center capital expenditure.

nodes.function: fund and develop AI-capable data centers
secure utility interconnection, generation, transmission, and substation capacity
transform voltage at utility and transmission layers with large power transformers
transform and distribute power inside the campus with three-phase distribution equipment
supply qualified grain-oriented electrical steel, copper, components, labor, assembly, and testing
manufacture, qualify, deliver, commission, and service transformer and switchgear systems

edges.workaround: alternate site, phased energization, onsite generation, storage, or load flexibility
redesign changes equipment mix but does not eliminate voltage transformation
qualified second source or redesigned specification
refurbishment, inventory, or alternate qualified supplier

system_map.category_control: C-009 prohibits merging campus distribution equipment with large power transformers.

role_screening.hypothesis: GEV can own relevant transformer and grid-equipment capacity.
Qualified material, labor, testing, and new manufacturing capacity can unlock supply.
Efficiency, onsite generation, storage, alternate siting, and network redesign may reduce or defer grid equipment needs.
Qualification, testing, engineering, and interconnection services can capture value without owning transformer capacity.
Utilities and data-center developers may absorb higher equipment cost or project delay.
ETN supplies broad electrical systems into data-center and utility end markets but is not verified pure transformer exposure.

non_compensating_gates.reason: Funded demand, electrical necessity, and observed supply tightness were independently supported.
Lead times supported near-term persistence, but dated 2026-2027 capacity additions prevent perpetual extrapolation.
The disclosed segment was too broad to verify transformer-specific revenue, unit economics, capital needs, and per-share FCF.
No cutoff-dated Tier A closing price was admitted, so the valuation anchor remained an explicit assumption.

constraint.verified_function: qualified voltage transformation and grid connection at several non-fungible power-system layers

resolution_paths.path: qualified second source or redesigned specification
alternate siting, phased energization, onsite generation, storage, or load flexibility

bridge: required grid and campus transformer units
capex, working capital, interest, and tax
fully diluted per-share free cash flow
rent_capture.verified_through: broad Electrification orders, revenue, margin, backlog, and company balance-sheet capacity
unverified_multipliers: transformer-only realized price and gross margin

valuation.interpretation: The scenarios are sensitivity arithmetic, not a verified market-price or company valuation. The transformer-only base FCF sensitivity is too small and too unverified to establish an expectation gap for the whole company.

catalysts.event: transformer-specific backlog, capacity, price, or mix disclosure
catalysts.thesis_effect: could complete or refute the rent-capture bridge

catalysts.event: announced GEV, Siemens Energy, and Hitachi Energy capacity milestones
catalysts.thesis_effect: tests both monetization and scarcity-resolution timing

catalysts.event: conversion of data-center requests into financed interconnection and procurement
catalysts.thesis_effect: separates funded load from speculative queue volume

strongest_countercase.case: The theme merges different transformer classes, extrapolates hyperscaler capital expenditure into supplier orders, and ignores capacity response; broad Electrification growth may be real without producing material AI-transformer FCF per GEV share.

kill_switches.condition: Two independent official sources show standard transformer delivery times at or below 12 months.
kill_switches.effect: reclassify the industry constraint as resolving

kill_switches.condition: Transformer-specific revenue and incremental FCF still cannot be bridged after the next two reporting cycles.
Verified data-center projects are not a material driver of qualified transformer orders.

portfolio_fit.conclusion: The two-name comparison behaves as one causal cluster, not two independent bets.

documented_negative_search: competing capacity and factory start dates
alternate siting, onsite generation, storage, load flexibility, and redesign
segment aggregation and missing transformer unit economics
capex, working capital, dilution, and per-share conversion
valuation anchor provenance and expectation-gap uncertainty
open_questions: What portion of GEV Electrification orders and RPO was transformer-specific at the cutoff?
What portion was attributable to verified AI data-center projects rather than broader grid investment?
What qualified transformer capacity, realized price, gross margin, capex, and working-capital path belonged to GEV?
Which transformer class and legal entity owned each data-center opportunity?
What cutoff-dated Tier A market price and matched benchmark should anchor a true expectation-gap test?
